# OpenClaw Security Self-Audit Guide
# Source: Attaché (https://docs.attache.dev/security)
# Version: 2026-03-19
# License: Apache 2.0
#
# This file is designed to be read by an OpenClaw agent to audit its own
# deployment. Feed it to your agent and ask: "Read this file and audit
# my configuration against these best practices."
#
# The agent should have access to its own openclaw.json config file
# (typically at ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json) and be able to run
# `openclaw --version` and `openclaw security audit`.

---

## How to use this file

You are an OpenClaw agent. This file describes security best practices for
your deployment. Read your own configuration file at ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json
and check each item below. Report findings as PASS, WARN, or FAIL with
specific remediation steps.

Run these commands to gather your configuration:
- `openclaw --version` (check version)
- `cat ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` (read config — redact tokens in output)
- `openclaw security audit --json` (built-in audit)
- `lsof -i -nP | grep ESTABLISHED` (network connections)
- `ls ~/.ssh/authorized_keys` (SSH keys)
- `crontab -l` (cron jobs)
- `ls ~/Library/LaunchAgents/` (macOS LaunchAgents)

---

## 1. Version and Patching

CRITICAL: Check `openclaw --version`

- FAIL if version < 2026.3.12 (multiple unpatched CVEs)
- WARN if version < latest stable (check https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/releases)
- Known vulnerable versions:
  - < 2026.1.29: CVE-2026-25253 (auth token exfiltration via crafted URL, CVSS 8.8)
  - < 2026.2.23: CVE-2026-22175 (exec allowlist bypass via busybox/toybox, CVSS 7.1)
  - < 2026.2.26: Advisory: DM-paired senders authorized in group contexts
  - < 2026.3.11: Advisory: WebSocket origin bypass in trusted-proxy auth mode (Critical)
  - < 2026.3.11: Advisory: Exec allowlist glob pattern `?` crosses directory boundaries
  - < 2026.3.12: Advisory: Setup/pairing codes embed long-lived gateway token

Remediation: `npm update -g openclaw && openclaw gateway restart`

---

## 2. Gateway Configuration

### 2a. Binding
Check: `gateway.bind` in openclaw.json

- PASS if "loopback" or "127.0.0.1"
- FAIL if "0.0.0.0" or missing (defaults to all interfaces on some versions)
- FAIL if any public IP address
- Context: 135,000-220,000+ OpenClaw instances found exposed to the internet

### 2b. Auth mode
Check: `gateway.auth.mode` in openclaw.json

- PASS if "token"
- FAIL if "trusted-proxy" (attack surface for origin bypass advisory)
- WARN if auth section is missing entirely

### 2c. Tailscale mode
Check: `gateway.tailscale.mode` in openclaw.json (if Tailscale is configured)

- PASS if "serve"
- FAIL if "funnel" (exposes gateway to public internet via Tailscale ingress)

---

## 3. Exec Security

CRITICAL: Check `exec.security` in openclaw.json

- PASS if "allowlist" or "ask"
- FAIL if "full" (any prompt injection gives attacker arbitrary command execution)
- WARN if allowlist contains broad patterns like `*`, `?`, or shell wrappers (busybox, toybox, sh, bash)
- WARN if `sudo` is on the allowlist
- WARN if `op` (1Password CLI) is directly on the allowlist without a proxy

Context: With exec.security: "full", there is no technical barrier between a
prompt injection and arbitrary command execution under the agent's OS user.
This is the single most impactful security setting.

Remediation: Switch to "allowlist" mode. Audit which commands your agent
actually uses by reviewing session logs, then build an explicit allowlist.

---

## 4. Channel Policies

### 4a. Slack
Check: `channels.slack.groupPolicy` and per-account settings

- PASS if "allowlist" or "pairing"
- WARN if "open" (anyone in any channel can trigger agent tools)
- Check per-account overrides: `channels.slack.accounts.*.groupPolicy`
- Check per-channel: `channels.slack.accounts.*.channels.*.requireMention`
- WARN if any channel has requireMention: false in a shared workspace

### 4b. Discord
Check: `channels.discord.groupPolicy` and guild settings

- PASS if "allowlist"
- WARN if individual guilds have allowlist overrides but top-level is "open"
- Check: `channels.discord.guilds.*.requireMention`

### 4c. General principle
All senders to one agent share the same delegated tool authority.
If the agent has exec access, everyone in a shared channel can trigger exec.
There is no per-sender permission scoping within a single agent.

WARN if any channel with groupPolicy "open" has an agent with exec access.

---

## 5. Credential Management

### 5a. Plaintext secrets in config
Check openclaw.json for any fields that look like API keys or tokens

- FAIL if API keys appear as plaintext values in the config file
- Specifically check: tools.web.search.apiKey, memorySearch.apiKey,
  and any custom tool configurations
- WARN if bot tokens or app tokens are in plaintext (even though they must be)
- Remediation: Move secrets to 1Password and reference via `op read`

### 5b. 1Password vault scoping
If using 1Password:

- WARN if the service account token has access to personal vaults
- Best practice: Dedicated "Agent Credentials" vault with only what the agent needs
- WARN if the agent can run `op` directly (should use a secrets proxy)

### 5c. GitHub tokens
Check for GitHub authentication configuration

- PASS if using GitHub App with installation tokens (short-lived, scoped)
- WARN if using fine-grained PAT (acceptable but check expiration)
- FAIL if using classic PAT (broad scope, no repository restrictions)

### 5d. Gateway token rotation
Check: `gateway.auth.token` age

- WARN if the gateway token has not been rotated since upgrading past 2026.3.12
  (setup codes in earlier versions contained the long-lived token)
- Remediation: `openclaw config set gateway.auth.token "$(openssl rand -hex 32)"`

---

## 6. Network Posture

### 6a. Inbound access
- PASS if gateway only accessible via localhost and Tailscale
- FAIL if gateway responds to connections from LAN IP
- Test: From another machine, `curl http://<mac-lan-ip>:18789/` should FAIL

### 6b. Outbound access
- WARN if no outbound network restrictions are configured
- Best practice: DNS-level blocking of known exfiltration endpoints
  or macOS pf firewall rules limiting which domains the agent can reach
- Check for unexpected outbound connections:
  `lsof -i -nP | grep ESTABLISHED | grep -v "localhost|127.0.0.1|::1|tailscale|100.64|100.100"`

---

## 7. Agent Isolation (Multiplayer)

If multiple agents or shared channels are in use:

### 7a. Personal vs team agent separation
- WARN if a single agent with full tool access operates in both DMs and shared channels
- Best practice: Personal agent (DMs, full tools) + team agent (shared channels, restricted tools)

### 7b. Memory isolation
- FAIL if a team-facing agent has access to personal memory files
  (MEMORY.md, SOUL.md, USER.md, daily notes)
- Best practice: Separate workspace directories for personal and team agents

### 7c. Tool restrictions for shared agents
Check agent-specific tool deny lists in openclaw.json

- WARN if a shared-channel agent has exec access
- WARN if a shared-channel agent has browser access
- WARN if a shared-channel agent can spawn sub-agents
- Best practice: Team agents should deny exec, browser, sessions_spawn, tts, nodes

---

## 8. Persistence and IOC Checks

### 8a. SSH keys
Check: `cat ~/.ssh/authorized_keys`

- WARN if any unrecognized keys are present
- Each key should map to a known device

### 8b. Scheduled tasks
Check: `crontab -l` and `ls ~/Library/LaunchAgents/`

- Expected LaunchAgents: ai.openclaw.gateway.plist (and optionally github-webhooks)
- WARN if any unexpected LaunchAgents or cron entries exist

### 8c. Suspicious exec patterns in logs
Search recent gateway logs for indicators of compromise:

- `grep -i "busybox\|toybox" ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log` (CVE-2026-22175)
- `grep -iE "/dev/tcp|nc -e|mkfifo|ncat |netcat " ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log` (reverse shells)
- `grep -iE "curl.*(pastebin|ngrok|webhook\.site|pipedream|requestbin)" ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log` (exfiltration)
- `grep -iE "base64 -d" ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log` (encoded payloads)

- FAIL if any of these patterns are found (investigate immediately)

### 8d. Config audit trail
Check: `tail -20 ~/.openclaw/logs/config-audit.jsonl`

- All entries should trace to intentional `openclaw config set` commands
- WARN if any entries have unexpected sources or suspicious patterns

---

## 9. LLM Provider Configuration

### 9a. Subscription tier
- FAIL if using consumer-tier subscriptions (Claude Free, ChatGPT Free/Plus)
  for any work involving client or sensitive data
- PASS if using API tier (Anthropic or OpenAI) — no training, short retention
- Best practice for sensitive clients: Anthropic Zero Data Retention agreement
  or AWS Bedrock (model provider never sees prompts)

### 9b. Provider data handling (verify periodically)
- Anthropic API: No training. 7-day retention (reduced Sept 2025). ZDR available.
- OpenAI API: No training (since March 2023). 30-day abuse monitoring.
- AWS Bedrock: No storage, no logging, provider has no access.
- Google Vertex AI: Per GCP data processing terms.

---

## 10. Log Management

### 10a. Log rotation
- WARN if gateway.err.log exceeds 1GB without rotation configured
- Best practice: Configure logrotate or launchd-based rotation
- Retain at least 90 days of logs for forensic purposes

### 10b. Log access
- WARN if log files are world-readable
- Best practice: Restrict log file permissions to the agent's OS user

---

## Scoring

After checking all items, calculate a score:

- Each FAIL = 0 points
- Each WARN = 1 point
- Each PASS = 2 points

Sections 1-4 are weighted 2x (most impactful security controls).

Report the total score, the maximum possible score, and a percentage.
List all FAIL items first with specific remediation commands.
List WARN items second with recommendations.

### Risk rating:
- 90-100%: Strong security posture
- 70-89%: Acceptable with noted improvements
- 50-69%: Significant gaps — address FAIL items immediately
- Below 50%: Critical — stop and harden before continued use

---

- Auth0 OpenClaw Security Guide: https://auth0.com/blog/five-step-guide-securing-moltbot-ai-agent/
